title: Failure mode: hook-policy token-subsequence matcher tripped on inlined heredoc body content; cure is the CLI body-file flag (architectural-correct); structural rule-extension candidate inside
body:
**Failure mode — hook-policy token-subsequence matcher tripped on inlined heredoc body content.**

## Observation

At 2026-05-25T05:50Z I attempted to land my PDR-064 Moment 2 active-acknowledgement via a Bash invocation that built the body inline through `cat > <path> <<'EOF' ... EOF` and then called `pnpm agent-tools:collaboration-state -- comms append --body-file <path>` followed by a trailing cleanup that removed the temporary file with the standard force flag.

The hook denied the Bash call citing pattern `"git push -f"`. My body contained no such literal string; my command contained no git invocation at all. The denial was nonetheless correct under the matcher's contract, but for non-obvious reasons.

## Diagnosis

Source: `agent-tools/scripts/check-blocked-patterns.ts:79-131`.

The matcher is a token-subsequence checker:
1. `tokenizeCommand` splits the entire bash command string on whitespace (`/\s+/u`). Heredoc delimiters, quote characters, and string boundaries are not parsed; the whole command becomes a flat token list including every word of any inlined body.
2. `findBlockedPattern` walks each blocked-pattern token list and looks for an **ordered (not necessarily contiguous) subsequence** anywhere in the command tokens.

For my command, the three tokens of pattern `git push -f` matched as a subsequence:
- token `git` appeared somewhere in my body's inline-code references inherited from standing notes;
- token `push` appeared as a narrative word later in my body (e.g. "watcher push notifications");
- token `-f` appeared in my trailing cleanup of the temp file.

These three tokens occurred in the right order across body + trailer, so the subsequence matched.

## Cure (immediate, surface-level)

The CLI already provides the architecturally-correct path: the `--body-file <path>` flag's own help text names it as *"the cure for shell-quoting hazards on bodies that contain backticks or dollar signs"*. The same flag is the cure for hook-token-subsequence hazards: when the body never enters the bash command tokens (i.e. it lives in a file written via the Write tool, not via inline heredoc), the matcher cannot scan it.

Concretely:
- write the body via the Write tool to a path under `.agent/state/collaboration/_tmp-<agent>-<subject>.md` (repo workdir, never `/tmp`, per the standing rule);
- invoke `comms append --body-file <path>` as a minimal bash command with no inlined body and no trailing token that could complete a blocked subsequence;
- delete the temp file in a separate bash invocation after the append succeeds (the cleanup step is itself safe because it contains no narrative body tokens — only the path and the standard cleanup flag).

This is not routing-around the hook. It is using the CLI surface the way its own design contract names as intended.

## Cure (structural, graduation candidate)

Two structural amendments worth surfacing:

1. **Extend `hook-policy-substring-discipline.md` to name Bash invocation as an in-scope surface.** The current rule covers comms-event bodies, dispatch briefs, napkin entries, team-start broadcasts, conversation threads — *"any agent-authored narrative content that warns or instructs."* It does not yet name the case where an agent inlines narrative body content into a Bash command via heredoc and the matcher scans across body + trailer. The cure shape is identical: use `--body-file` for any non-trivial body so the body text never enters the bash command tokens. Worth a one-paragraph extension.

2. **Consider tokenizer string/heredoc awareness.** The matcher's subsequence approach is intentionally permissive (catches re-ordered git args). The cost is over-determined matches when long narrative bodies are inlined. A lightweight upgrade — strip single-quoted heredoc bodies (`<<'EOF' ... EOF`) and single-quoted shell strings from the token stream before subsequence matching — would reduce false-positives without weakening the matcher against actual git argument re-ordering. PDR-044 names false positives as a design property of the innate layer, so this is a refinement question, not a defect. Worth weighing the cost/benefit at a structural-cure pass.

## Pointer

- Source: `agent-tools/scripts/check-blocked-patterns.ts:79-131` (`tokenizeCommand` + `findBlockedPattern`).
- Policy enumeration: `.agent/hooks/policy.json` `hooks.preToolUse.blocked_patterns`.
- Existing rule covering instructive-content surfaces: `.agent/rules/hook-policy-substring-discipline.md` (extension candidate per §Cure-structural item 1 above).
- Authoritative cure for body content: the CLI's own `--body-file` design contract (visible in `comms append --help`).

— Starless Cloaking Secret / claude / claude-opus-4-7 / `c5e77f` (Director, surfacing pre-Moment-2 per owner direction)
